channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Beacontn] Venti-Sized Subsidy
item.description: Tennessee taxpayers are about to spend millions on a Starbucks order they didn’t ask for. In April, the world’s largest coffee chain announced it would be opening a Southeast corporate office in Music City.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Billionaires Are Not The Problem
item.description:
“You can’t earn a billion dollars. You just can’t earn that. You can get market power. You can break rules. You can do all sorts of things. You can abuse labor laws. You can pay people less than what they’re worth. But you can’t earn that.” These comments made by Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez on a […]

The post Billionaires Are Not The Problem appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] The Defeat of Measure 120 is a Win for the Oregon System
item.description:
By Naomi Inman It’s election time in Oregon and the Oregonian editorial board...

The post The Defeat of Measure 120 is a Win for the Oregon System appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jbartlett] Manchester school board elevates educator staffing above all other priorities
item.description:
With Manchester’s school district looking to trim its proposed 2027 budget to stay within the city’s tax cap, the Board of School Committee took the extraordinary step of forbidding the district from achieving a balanced budget by laying off educators. The move effectively elevates raw educator staffing numbers over all other district priorities, even though […]

The post Manchester school board elevates educator staffing above all other priorities appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Beacontn] Beacon Applauds Department of Labor Decision to Withdraw Unlawful Overtime Rule
item.description: Today, the U.S. Department of Labor will formally publish a final rule overturning the Biden administration’s 2024 overtime rule after multiple federal courts vacated the rule nationwide and the department declined to continue defending it on appeal.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF talks to KOAT 7: What ever happened to taxpayers-subsidized Unser Crossing?
item.description:
New Mexico has a long history of spending taxpayer dollars on failed developments that don’t pan out. Going back nearly 20 years to the Unser Crossing development (originally slated for Unser and Central) was one such plan. Of course, RGF loves economic development. We want to see jobs and businesses locate to our state, but […]

The post RGF talks to KOAT 7: What ever happened to taxpayers-subsidized Unser Crossing? appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Foropportunity] How Georgia’s Governor Impacts Your Daily Life: Understanding the Role and the Election Process
item.description:
From vetoing bills to deciding the budget, check out five ways Georgia’s governor impacts schools, safety, work, and public programs that our families and communities rely on.

The post How Georgia’s Governor Impacts Your Daily Life: Understanding the Role and the Election Process appeared first on For Opportunity.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Unleashing Hope via K–12 Scholarships for Families: Why Rhode Island Should Opt-In to Federal Education Tax Credits
item.description:
Rhode Island students deserve better. The federal Education Freedom Tax Credit (EFTC) empowers working and middle-class families with scholarships for K-12 private schools, charters, homeschooling, tutoring & more at zero cost to RI taxpayers or public schools. The governor can opt-in today for January 2027 launch. Give Ocean State families real choice and brighter futures. Don’t leave millions in education funding on the table.

The post Unleashing Hope via K–12 Scholarships for Families: Why Rhode Island Should Opt-In to Federal Education Tax Credits appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Arkansaspolicyfoundation] SURPLUS REVENUES FUEL INCOME TAX CUTS
item.description: (May 2026) One sentence in an annual state report explains how policymakers have achieved a serious fiscal goal: reduction of the top state individual income tax rate under three governors and a succession of Republican legislatures. “(Arkansas) revenues are expanding because the business cycle’s current state is expansion, not contraction.” Policy Foundation research memo, Nov. […]

item.title: [Arkansaspolicyfoundation] TOP ARKANSAS INCOME TAX RATE CUT TO 3.7%
item.description: (May 2026) Arkansas policymakers reduced the top state individual income tax rate from 3.9% to 3.7% in early May, using an estimated $334.4 million projected fiscal surplus in the current 2025-26 fiscal year. Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders called a special session of the state legislature April 30 to consider the tax cut. The session convened […]

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH offers the lowest gas and grocery prices in New England this Memorial Day Weekend
item.description:
Granite Staters can expect to have the most affordable Memorial Day Weekend in New England this year, as New Hampshire offers the region’s lowest gas and grocery prices, a Josiah Bartlett Center for Public Policy review of regional prices shows.  Memorial Day weekend kicks off both summer travel season and outdoor grilling season, and this […]

The post NH offers the lowest gas and grocery prices in New England this Memorial Day Weekend appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] New York Says “Yes” to Scholarships–Oregon Should Too
item.description:
By Kathryn Hickok Governor Kathy Hochul announced May 5 that she intends to...

The post New York Says “Yes” to Scholarships–Oregon Should Too appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Tallahassee Democrat: Levy Cap: Florida’s Smartest Option for Property Tax Reform
item.description:
Florida needs property tax reform that is sustainable and honest about trade-offs. Of the options now under consideration,...

The post Tallahassee Democrat: Levy Cap: Florida’s Smartest Option for Property Tax Reform appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] Rep. Otis Anthony: Can the Delta Rise Again?
item.description:
Rep. Otis Anthony: Can the Delta Rise Again? On this episode of the Empower Podcast, State Rep. Otis Anthony joins Grant for a wide-ranging conversation about poverty, work, education, economic development, and the future of the Mississippi Delta. Anthony shares how growing up in Indianola shaped his view of public service, why he believes the Delta’s…

The post Rep. Otis Anthony: Can the Delta Rise Again? first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Libertas] Libertas Network Acquires Family Brand to Strengthen Intentional Families
item.description:
Libertas Network’s acquisition of Praxis ignites a bold revolution in education by empowering young innovators to bypass outdated systems and chart their own path to success.

The post Libertas Network Acquires Family Brand to Strengthen Intentional Families appeared first on Libertas Network: Changing Hearts, Minds, and Laws.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Freedom Foundation launches website to educate public employees about union alternatives
item.description:
EmpoweredEmployees.org gives workers clear information on how to remove or replace unwanted labor unions.

The post Freedom Foundation launches website to educate public employees about union alternatives appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] CALIFORNIA: IHSS providers tricked into staying, overpaying
item.description:
We hear it all too often from In-Home Support Services (IHSS) care providers considering opting out of their union: “If I do, I’ll lose my dental insurance.”

The post CALIFORNIA: IHSS providers tricked into staying, overpaying appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] AICOA Would Pull Up the Ladder on Florida’s Small Businesses
item.description:
Washington didn’t build Florida’s 3.5 million small businesses. Low taxes, light regulation, and an entrepreneurial culture built an economy where 3.8 million Floridians...

The post AICOA Would Pull Up the Ladder on Florida’s Small Businesses appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] UPSKILL: Putting the state's needs first, avoiding big questions
item.description:
Lawmakers across the country often insist on making “investments” in economic programs to help fill a perceived need for skilled workers. But such efforts often put the needs of the state and favored industries ahead of the needs of individuals -- and they miss big picture questions in the process.  This critical view should be […]

The post UPSKILL: Putting the state's needs first, avoiding big questions appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] Opinion piece: SPEED Act would be a win for Left and Right
item.description:
The following appeared in the Eastern New Mexico News on May 5, 2026, and in several other New Mexico media outlets. Federal legislation known as the Standardizing Permitting and Expediting Economic Development (SPEED Act) would make needed improvements to America’s antiquated federal permitting system for energy projects. While Sen. Martin Heinrich and the Rio Grande […]

The post Opinion piece: SPEED Act would be a win for Left and Right appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF President discusses AI data centers on “Powering America” podcast
item.description:
In the latest episode of the Powering America Podcast, Paul Gessing, president of the Rio Grande Foundation, discusses data centers and energy policy in New Mexico. Gessing highlights the state’s potential for data center development due to its natural resources and stable environment, while also addressing the challenges posed by political opposition and environmental concerns. The […]

The post RGF President discusses AI data centers on “Powering America” podcast appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.description:
John A. Charles, Jr. Portland Public Schools (PPS) held a ground-breaking ceremony in...

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] 87% of BOTA property tax appeals granted or partially resolved in taxpayers’ favor
item.description: Some Kansas legislators opposed property tax assessment limits because they believe there is nothing wrong with appraisals—even House Speaker Dan Hawkins said, “property appraisals are correct“—but Board of Tax Appeals (BOTA) resolutions reveal a massive problem. Data we received from an Open Records request show that 87% of appeals over the last six years were granted […]

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine Taxpayers Are Once Again Paying for Bellows’ Fight with Trump
item.description:
After a controversial Bureau of Motor Vehicles decision, the Maine taxpayers are once again being dragged into a high-profile constitutional fight with the Trump administration. The U.S. Department of Justice has sued the State of Maine, Governor Janet Mills, Attorney General Aaron Frey, and Secretary of State Shenna Bellows over a Bureau of Motor Vehicles […]

The post Maine Taxpayers Are Once Again Paying for Bellows’ Fight with Trump appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Freedom Foundation hails Louisiana’s new worker-protection bill
item.description:
The Freedom Foundation today applauded the Louisiana Legislature for passing Senate Bill 312, a measure that strengthens worker freedom by ensuring employees understand their rights before joining a union. 

The post Freedom Foundation hails Louisiana’s new worker-protection bill appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Mspolicy] How Good is Your Kid's School? A Clarion Ledger Exclusive
item.description: The post How Good is Your Kid's School? A Clarion Ledger Exclusive appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] The Bottom-Up Case for Regulating AI Chatbots
item.description:
When looking to regulate emerging technologies, the popular instinct has been to draft these rules from the top...

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Artificial Intelligence Takes Hurricane Season by Storm
item.description:
Hurricane season began this week, on June 1, and stretches through November 30th. For Louisiana, the season has become part of routine life and its residents are no stranger to both the minor annoyances and major tragic upheavals that accompany the storms. Developments in artificial intelligence (AI) are rapidly changing the way states prepare for,...

The post Artificial Intelligence Takes Hurricane Season by Storm appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.description:
by Wells Winegar, Executive Director   On Thursday, the Assembly Environment and Solid Waste Committee will consider A3735, the so-called […]

item.title: [Beacontn] First Principles Series: Don’t Ban Data Centers. Don’t Subsidize Them, Either.
item.description: We can allow data centers to exist while keeping the courts open for nuisance lawsuits against bad actors who infringe upon protections like water rights when other property owners are actually harmed by them.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Unconstitutional Programs and the Administrative State: Stopping the 1419 Project
item.description: Over the past century, political progressives have built a bureaucratic machine with the goal of undermining our republic. It accomplishes this by making people dependent on government programs and by ignoring the will of the people and their elected representatives, ruling solely through executive fiat. Idaho has long been at the forefront of the battle […]

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] A Win for Worker Freedom in Louisiana! SB312 Awaits Gov. Landry’s Signature
item.description:
A big win for worker freedom in Louisiana! Senate Bill 312, sponsored by Senator Kirk Talbot and Representative Mike Melerine, is on its way to Governor Jeff Landry’s desk. This bill gives public sector workers more control over their paychecks by: • Allowing union members to resign their union membership and stop paying dues at...

The post A Win for Worker Freedom in Louisiana! SB312 Awaits Gov. Landry’s Signature appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraska’s Property Tax “Caps” Are Not the Same as Iowa’s
item.description:
In the summer of 2024, Nebraska lawmakers gathered for a special legislative session focused almost entirely on property tax reform. After years of frustration from taxpayers watching valuations and tax bills rise faster than incomes, lawmakers promised meaningful action to slow the growth of property taxes.  The result was LB34 designed to place new limits...

The post Nebraska’s Property Tax “Caps” Are Not the Same as Iowa’s appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] California’s “Wealth” Tax is Coming for Everyone
item.description:
If you own property in California, you’re not safe. A new ballot measure will empower the state to confiscate a percentage of the assets of any resident, even though its initial provisions don’t communicate that intent. California’s “One-Time Wealth Tax for State-Funded Healthcare, Education, and Food Assistance Programs Initiative,” which has already qualified for the November ballot,...

The post California’s “Wealth” Tax is Coming for Everyone first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Five Billion Barrels of Crude Oil
item.description:
There is no realistic scenario imaginable that does not include Californians consuming at least another five billion barrels of oil before the state achieves its much touted official goal of a “clean energy future.” Here are the numbers: In 2025, Californians consumed 484 million barrels of crude oil. To understand why another five billion barrels is the...

The post Five Billion Barrels of Crude Oil first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Jbartlett] BET cuts for the long run
item.description:
As the 2026 legislative session wound down, it looked as though Republicans wouldn’t be able to agree on one of the party’s signature issues: cutting business taxes. During committee of conference negotiations last week, word spread (and was even reported in the news) that a House-passed Business Enterprise Tax cut was dead.  House and Senate […]

The post BET cuts for the long run appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] What Rural Nebraska Is Losing
item.description:
NOTE: Before he left, our previous intern provided a blog post based on a paper he’d written for school, discussing the challenges of big cities (especially Omaha) in our state. For his inaugural post, our current intern, Ethan Roth, has provided a colorful discussion of some of the challenges in rural Nebraska below, based on...

The post What Rural Nebraska Is Losing appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory
item.description:
Despite common-sense energy reforms suggested by Governor McKee; despite pledged reforms by all other New England states; and despite indisputable research that shows how Rhode Island’s net zero emissions energy strategy will lead to unaffordable costs and dangerous risks … the budget put forth by the new Speaker of the RI House of Representative, Christopher Blazejewski … doubles-down on the state’s radical Act On Climate mandates.

The post Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.description:

item.description:
Public school district leaders are thinking about what scholarships from private donations through a new federal tax credit program the will do for their students.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Property Tax Relief Options Explained
item.description: The most important point to understand regarding property taxes is that they are budget-driven. This simply means local taxing districts, which include counties, cities, schools, fire districts, etc., set property tax budgets, and those budgets are the primary driver of property tax payments and the changes (usually increases) over time. Long-term property tax relief requires […]

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansas Farmers Need an Economy That Can Withstand a Bad Crop Year
item.description: Kansas wheat country is facing a hard year. Drought has damaged winter wheat across the Plains, and the latest crop tour estimates point to a much smaller harvest than last year. That should matter to every Kansas policymaker, not just farmers. For many farm families in Kansas, this is not an abstract policy discussion. A […]

item.description:

item.description: A new Hartford court filing is reviving scrutiny of City Council President Thomas “TJ” Clarke II and the city’s handling of a sexual harassment controversy that ended in a taxpayer-funded […]

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: A BSU Prof’s Defense of Islam CERTAIN PUBLIC POSITIONS are so poorly reasoned that it is best to let them just sit — let them sit moldering as events unfold. Such is the widely published defense of Islam by Dr. Michael Hicks, the George and Frances Ball Distinguished Professor of Economics at Ball State University and director of its Center Read the full article...

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] The men who wrote the Declaration of Independence
item.description: The job of drafting a declaration of independence was handed to a group historians call the “committee of five.”

item.title: [Itrfoundation] How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 1
item.description:
States are moving in opposite directions on income taxes. Some states, including Washington, Hawaii, Maine, California, and potentially Colorado, are pursuing wealth taxes or higher rates on top earners, while others are reducing or eliminating income taxes. Tax-cutting states are emphasizing competitiveness and growth. South Carolina, Arkansas, West Virginia, Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, and Utah all enacted income tax reductions in 2026, with several adopting revenue triggers that automatically lower rates when state finances allow. Some states are seeking constitutional taxpayer protections. Iowa, Missouri, and North Carolina are advancing constitutional amendments designed to make future tax increases more difficult and provide longer-term safeguards against higher income taxes.

The post How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 1 appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] Congress Introduces Right to Try for Individualized Treatments Act to Expand Patients’ Access to Gene Therapies
item.description:
Patients desperately waiting to access cutting-edge, life-saving treatments have new hope today with the introduction of the Right to Try..

The post Congress Introduces Right to Try for Individualized Treatments Act to Expand Patients’ Access to Gene Therapies appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Pioneer Institute Urges Caution as Legislature Considers New Restrictions on Ballot Initiatives
item.description:
As Massachusetts lawmakers consider proposals that would make it more difficult for citizens to place questions before voters, Pioneer Institute is urging caution.

Pioneer Institute believes that policy change should occur primarily through the legislative process. However, the initiative petition process remains an important safety valve when citizens believe elected officials are not addressing their concerns.

The post Pioneer Institute Urges Caution as Legislature Considers New Restrictions on Ballot Initiatives appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] 5 new laws starting July 1
item.description:
A list of laws Gov. J.B. Pritzker signed over a year ago take effect July 1. Here’s what you need to know.

The post 5 new laws starting July 1 appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Transportation officials may be pivoting as costs explode on interstate bridge replacement
item.description: It's likely that rail costs are being severely underestimated and will comprise a much larger share of the total budget than projected.

item.title: [Mspolicy] More Money Won’t Fix Our Schools. Our Data Proves It
item.description:
For decades, America has been told that the key to better schools is more money. Underperformance, the argument runs, is really a question of resources. Just give the teacher unions what they ask for, and good outcomes will follow. Mississippi is starting to show that this simply is not true. Over the past decade, Mississippi […]

The post More Money Won’t Fix Our Schools. Our Data Proves It appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.description:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE Media Contact:  sydney@pelicaninstitute.org  |  504-717-1606 June 9, 2026 New Law Protects Public Employees’ First Amendment Rights and Ends Automatic Dues Collection BATON ROUGE, LA — Workers for Opportunity, a national organization dedicated to advancing workplace freedom for employees across the country, applauds the Louisiana legislature for passing—and Gov. Jeff Landry for signing—SB...

The post Louisiana Legislature and Governor Secure Landmark Victory for Worker Freedom appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.description:
Circuit Court – Adams County

The post Circuit Court – Adams County appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Coverage vs. Care (Part 1)
item.description: On Valentine’s Day 1966, Gov. Bob Smylie called a special session of the state Legislature to deal, primarily, with reapportioning the Idaho Senate to comply with the U.S. Supreme Court’s one-man-one-vote requirement. Back then, there was a senator for each of the state’s 44 counties, and the courts had ruled the arrangement unconstitutional. So when […]

item.title: [Inpolicy] Franke: The Pledge of Allegiance
item.description: by Mark Franke I saw a recent news report of a school classroom in Tennessee that got a very practical lesson in why America has an official Pledge of Allegiance.  A substitute teacher was surprised that most students remained seated during the school’s recitation of the Pledge. Confused, he asked them why. Most couldn’t provide Read the full article...

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Illinois gas tax set to increase every year—without a vote
item.description:
Gov. J.B. Pritzker’s 2019 “Rebuild Illinois” plan created automatic inflationary adjustments in the state gas tax, which could reach over $1 per gallon by 2056.

The post Illinois gas tax set to increase every year—without a vote appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 2
item.description:
States are increasingly divided between two fiscal philosophies: one focused on higher spending supported by higher taxes, and another centered on spending restraint, tax competitiveness, and economic growth. High-tax states such as Illinois and Minnesota continue to face budget pressures and are pursuing new taxes and revenue sources, while states like Iowa and Arkansas are using spending discipline to support ongoing income tax reductions. The debate over taxes is ultimately a debate over the role of government, with states increasingly choosing between higher spending and lower taxes.

The post How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 2 appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells’ $368,885 Secret Meeting
item.description:
For Immediate Release: June 10, 2026 Contact: press@reforminggovernment.org   IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells $368,885 Secret Meeting Lawsuit argues DPI violated open meetings laws, seeks to invalidate DPI’s secret meeting that weakened Wisconsin testing standards Delafield, Wis. — The Institute for Reforming Government (IRG), represented by the Wisconsin Transparency Project, is taking the Department […]

The post IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells’ $368,885 Secret Meeting appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Sues USDA Over Unconstitutional Milk Tax
item.description:
WILL filed a federal lawsuit against the USDA and the National Dairy Promotion and Research Board (Dairy Board) on behalf of three Wisconsin dairy farmers. The lawsuit challenges the unconstitutional Dairy Checkoff program, which forces dairy farmers to fund private, third-party organizations that promote radical and ideologically driven Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) agendas.

The post WILL Sues USDA Over Unconstitutional Milk Tax appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] Connecticut’s Fiscal Transparency Problem
item.description: Connecticut officials often present the state as a model of fiscal discipline, pointing to budget surpluses, pension payments, fiscal rules and bond-rating upgrades as evidence that Hartford has turned a […]

item.title: [Will Law] Swan v Rollins
item.description:
This lawsuit is brought by three Wisconsin dairy farmers forced to subsidize the Dairy Checkoff: a burdensome federal program that leads to the demise of small farms under the guise of environmental “sustainability.” Not only is this compelled subsidy Orwellian, but it also violates the First Amendment to the United States Constitution and the Administrative Procedure Act.

The post Swan v Rollins appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] Jake Curtis | Point Taken
item.description: The post Jake Curtis | Point Taken appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraskanomics: Laura Ebke on Second Chances in Nebraska
item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome Platte Institute Senior Fellow Laura Ebke. During the episode Laura and Platte CEO Jim Vokal discuss a new short documentary from the Platte Institute highlighting the impact of the second chances legislation that was a part of LB 16 passed in 2024.  “Second Chance:...

The post Nebraskanomics: Laura Ebke on Second Chances in Nebraska appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Will Law] Victory: Pittsville School District Admits Parent’s Social Media Post Was Protected Speech, Settles First Amendment Dispute
item.description:
WILL secured a settlement agreement with the School District of Pittsville to vindicate the First Amendment rights of Amanda Vogel, a parent who received litigation threats from school district officials and visits from law enforcement following a social media post.

The post Victory: Pittsville School District Admits Parent’s Social Media Post Was Protected Speech, Settles First Amendment Dispute appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] An Alternative Vision for the Potter Valley Project
item.description:
A recent article in Grist characterized recent efforts by the Trump administration to preserve Scott Dam as a “culture war crusade.” That is accurate, so long as we recognize that efforts to demolish the dam – and diminish the value of the entire Potter Valley Project that the dam is a part of – are also the result of a culture...

The post An Alternative Vision for the Potter Valley Project first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] The Border War Truce’s Predictable (and Predicted) Problem
item.description:
Listen to this article When Missouri and Kansas agreed to a border war truce in 2019, the agreement was widely celebrated as the end of an expensive and counterproductive competition. […]

The post The Border War Truce’s Predictable (and Predicted) Problem appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] AI Job Displacement and Employer Hesitancy: What Massachusetts Data Reveals
item.description:
Currently, Tufts University’s American AI Jobs Risk Index projects that 9.3 million U.S. jobs are at risk of displacement in the next two-to-five years, with an associated $757 billion in household income at risk. The study focuses particularly, on “Wired Belt” metros, tech-heavy, knowledge-industry hubs such as Silicon Valley, Washington D.C., New York, and university towns like Durham-Chapel Hill and Boulder, which the researchers identify as most vulnerable to AI-driven job loss. With new technology comes new behaviors in the employment market, and employers are readjusting to this innovation.

The post AI Job Displacement and Employer Hesitancy: What Massachusetts Data Reveals appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] They’re Still Counting . . . and Counting
item.description:
This week, Tim and Matt discuss the seemingly endless California election count and the statewide races that are finally coming into focus.  Plus, they discuss the race for Los Angeles mayor and whether Spencer Pratt was cheated out of a spot in the November election.

The post They’re Still Counting . . . and Counting appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Unauthorized GLP-1 Knockoffs Risk Patient Safety and Future Innovation, New PRI Brief Warns
item.description:
As federal regulators intensify enforcement against companies selling unauthorized knockoffs of blockbuster weight-loss drugs, a new Pacific Research Institute brief warns that the booming GLP-1 knockoff market is creating patient safety risks while undermining future breakthrough medicines. “GLP-1s are among the most promising medical breakthroughs in decades, helping patients struggling with diabetes, obesity and other […]

The post Unauthorized GLP-1 Knockoffs Risk Patient Safety and Future Innovation, New PRI Brief Warns appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Weiss could oversee CNN editorial operations
item.description:
Ariel Zilber writes for the New York Post about a significant development in the legacy media. CBS News boss Bari Weiss is likely to gain editorial oversight of CNN if and when Paramount Skydance’s acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery is approved, according to a report. Paramount executives are said to have held preliminary discussions with...

The post Weiss could oversee CNN editorial operations appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Hanson pans California’s ongoing election problems
item.description:
The Daily Signal highlights an interview with Californian Victor Davis Hanson about his state’s electoral woes. The theft or the alleged theft or the irregularities are not actually in the counting. They’re in the mail-in ballots. And in California, almost any time you come in contact with a state agency, you’re going to … And all you have to...

The post Hanson pans California’s ongoing election problems appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] Civility is infrastructure we cannot allow to collapse
item.description: Without civil standards, even strong institutions begin to weaken

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Is California Coastal Commission finally getting its comeuppance?
item.description:
California has one of the world’s most spectacular coastlines, which meanders 1,100 miles from Imperial Beach to Crescent City. And, of course, everyone wants to “Save Our Coast” and assure public access to beaches, which is why Californians voted 55% to 45% in 1972 for Proposition 20. It promised to protect open space and restore habitats […]

The post Is California Coastal Commission finally getting its comeuppance? appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Illinois lawmakers fail to pass millionaires tax, path to progressive income tax
item.description:
None of the three proposed amendments to the Illinois Constitution that would have repealed or endangered the state’s flat income tax protection advanced during this year’s legislative session.

The post Illinois lawmakers fail to pass millionaires tax, path to progressive income tax appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Keating: A Rich Man’s Country?
item.description: by Maryann O. Keating, Ph.D. Democrats and Republicans tend not to discuss politics at social events. Recently, however, conversation at a local restaurant converged into two shared concerns: growing income inequality and oligarchy, the excessive influence of the wealthy.   Oligarchies concentrate power in the hands of elites who direct government policy for their own Read the full article...

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] Missouri’s Stalled Education Reforms with Cory Koedel
item.description:
Show-Me Institute · Missouri’s Stalled Education Reforms with Cory Koedel Susan Pendergrass speaks with Cory Koedel, director of education policy at the Show-Me Institute, about Missouri education policy following the […]

The post Missouri’s Stalled Education Reforms with Cory Koedel appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] States are getting serious about Medicaid fraud
item.description: Recently, Washington state and Idaho reported success in aggressively pursuing fraud in their respective Medicaid programs.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] MN teacher prep programs better aligned to science of reading but full picture more complicated
item.description:
A new analysis by the National Council on Teacher Quality (NCTQ) gave 93 percent of 15 elementary teacher preparation programs in Minnesota an “A” or “A+” for adequately covering the five core components of scientifically based reading instruction. This is a dramatic improvement from 2023, when only 14 percent met that bar. But a look...

The post MN teacher prep programs better aligned to science of reading but full picture more complicated appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] State Education Board Should Support Proposed Literature List
item.description:
The Texas State Board of Education (SBOE) should adopt a required literature list that supports the literacy and knowledge-acquisition of Texas students and not allow leftist organizations like the Texas Council of Teachers of English Language Arts (TCTELA) to pressure them into adopting a watered-down, politically driven list. Texas public schools have failed at both...

The post State Education Board Should Support Proposed Literature List first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Public Safety Starts With Solving Crimes, Not Just Reporting Them
item.description:
When a violent crime is committed, it tends to feel not just unwarranted but random. When a murder, sex crime, or burglary happens, we often ask, “How did this happen?” Even worse is when a violent crime is committed by someone with a violent criminal past. We then demand to know, “How did this happen… again?”  ...

The post Public Safety Starts With Solving Crimes, Not Just Reporting Them first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] Michigan’s Government-Driven Internet Expansion Efforts are Expensive and Ineffective
item.description: Study reveals subsidies and government-run networks have done little to close Michigan’s coverage gap

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansans Need Facts about Data Centers
item.description: My earlier Kansas Policy Institute piece, “Kansas Should Welcome AI Growth, Not Zone It Away”, drew a lot of feedback. Much of it came down to four concerns: water, electricity, zoning, and whether data centers really help local communities. Those are fair questions. Big projects often face scrutiny, and Kansans deserve answers about how new […]

item.title: [Johnlocke] Op-Ed: North Carolina and Virginia should declare a gerrymandering truce
item.description:
This op-ed originally appeared in the Richmond Times-Dispatch and the Greensboro News & Record. It is reprinted here with their permission. 2026 has become an unexpectedly fierce mid-decade continuation of America’s ongoing gerrymandering war. North Carolina and Virginia are both battlegrounds in that war, but there is a way they can call a cease-fire on...

The post Op-Ed: North Carolina and Virginia should declare a gerrymandering truce appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Critics mock the new White House ballroom, but modern security is no joke
item.description:
Every administration has a responsibility to adapt to the realities of the moment. This year’s White House Correspondents’ Dinner should have been the same as any other: a beloved, annual tradition that brings together the media, politicians, and administration officials to celebrate our free press, build relationships, and honor the First Amendment. Instead, the event served as...

The post Critics mock the new White House ballroom, but modern security is no joke first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Coming to a Classroom Near You: Removing geography, adding colonialism
item.description:
New social studies standards are coming to all Minnesota K-12 public schools this fall. According to the Minnesota Department of Education, the revised standards and benchmarks reflect a “shift away from standards that list a set of content students are expected to know” to conceptual learning and narratives. The biggest structural change in the 2021...

The post Coming to a Classroom Near You: Removing geography, adding colonialism appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Massachusetts Ranks First in K-12 Education–But Not for Long
item.description:
Since 2005, Massachusetts has achieved the top ranking in the biannual National Assessment of Educational Progress’s (NAEP) fourth-grade reading assessment. However, the Commonwealth’s lower quality Common Core-based English Language Arts (ELA) standards and lack of quality of reading pedagogy and curriculum at the local level are coming back to haunt it.

The post Massachusetts Ranks First in K-12 Education–But Not for Long appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Mackinac] Michigan should not punish short-term rentals
item.description: Lawmakers debate a variety of penalties

item.title: [Americanexperiment] A look at Minnesota’s new Clemency Review Commission
item.description:
The Minnesota Clemency Review Commission (CRC) was created by the DFL led Minnesota Legislature in 2023.  The Commission’s role is to review applications for clemency from convicted individuals and make recommendations to the Minnesota Board of Pardons (BOP). The CRC provides this description of clemency on its website: Clemency is any act of mercy or...

The post A look at Minnesota’s new Clemency Review Commission appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Food co-op seen as viable, more likely option than government-funded grocery store in Milwaukee
item.description:
The solution to problems left by grocery store closures in Milwaukee may include a civil-society option such as a food co-op, observers say.

The post Food co-op seen as viable, more likely option than government-funded grocery store in Milwaukee appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed
item.description:
Maine voters went to the polls on June 9, but in several of the state’s most important primary races, Election Day did not actually produce a winner. Instead, both gubernatorial primaries and the Democratic primary in Maine’s 2nd Congressional District are headed into ranked-choice voting tabulation. As a result, Mainers are left waiting days after […]

The post Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Facts to help you decide whether Wisconsin children should be eligible for donor-funded education scholarships
item.description:
What Wisconsinites need to know about how the Federal Scholarship Tax Credit program works.

The post Facts to help you decide whether Wisconsin children should be eligible for donor-funded education scholarships appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 3
item.description:
Colorado's Initiative 195 would replace the state's 4.4% flat income tax with a graduated income tax and significantly increase the amount of revenue the state can retain and spend. The proposal represents one of the most significant challenges to Colorado's Taxpayer Bill of Rights (TABOR), a constitutional limit on taxes and spending that has returned more than $12 billion to taxpayers since 1992. The debate highlights a broader national divide over fiscal policy, pitting advocates of higher taxes and government spending against supporters of taxpayer protections, spending limits, and voter control over tax increases.

The post How States Are Reshaping Income Tax Policy in 2026- Part 3 appeared first on ITR Foundation.
